docs.title: Harshil Shah — Engineering Playbook
docs.text: Practical engineering principles, delivery patterns, and architecture guidance shaped by 14 years of building software across regulated platforms, enterprise systems, and modern cloud applications.

docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
Hello, I'm Harshil Shah, a Senior Full Stack Engineer focused on building reliable, maintainable, and scalable software. My core experience spans .NET Core, C#, Python, React, Azure, and AWS, with a delivery background that combines engineering excellence, product thinking, and operational pragmatism.

This playbook is my personal reference for the standards I expect from production systems: clean design, thoughtful architecture, strong code review culture, and sustainable engineering habits.

docs.title: Why this playbook exists
docs.text:
I created this site for three reasons:

  1. Capture durable engineering principles that stay useful across teams, domains, and technology stacks.
  2. Document repeatable patterns for system design, code quality, and technical decision-making.
  3. Share a working reference that reflects how I approach software as a senior engineer targeting Senior, Lead, and Staff engineering roles in the UK, Netherlands, Germany, and Ireland.

docs.title: What this playbook covers
docs.text:
This playbook is designed as a practical operating manual for day-to-day engineering work.

Area What you will find Why it matters SOLID Principles Clear guidance on applying object-oriented design principles in production code Improves maintainability and extensibility System Design Patterns for scalability, reliability, observability, and service boundaries Supports better technical decisions at scale Python Best Practices Advice on readability, testing, packaging, and automation Keeps Python systems clean and production ready Code Review Review checklists, quality gates, and collaboration principles Raises code quality while strengthening teams ADRs Lightweight architectural decision records and templates Preserves context behind important decisions Engineering Principles Core beliefs about ownership, simplicity, performance, and delivery Aligns technical execution with professional standards About My background, achievements, and current focus Adds context to the perspective behind this playbook

docs.title: How to use this playbook
docs.text:
You can use this site in whichever way best matches your workflow:

  • As a reference when designing a service, reviewing code, or defining engineering standards.
  • As a coaching tool for helping teams align on maintainability, design quality, and delivery discipline.
  • As an interview preparation resource for conversations around architecture, trade-offs, engineering leadership, and software craftsmanship.
  • As a personal benchmark for the technical bar I aim to uphold in every project.

Suggested reading path

Start with Engineering Principles, then move to SOLID Principles and Code Review, and finally use System Design and ADRs for architecture-focused decision making.

docs.title: A personal note on continuous learning
docs.text:
The most valuable engineers I have worked with combine curiosity with consistency. They keep learning, but they also keep shipping. This playbook reflects that mindset. It is not a fixed statement of perfection; it is a living body of lessons from real systems, real trade-offs, and a long-term commitment to getting better at the craft of engineering.

If you are building software that needs to be reliable, understandable, and adaptable, I hope this playbook gives you something useful to take into your next design review, sprint, or production release.

docs.title: Professional profile
docs.text:
I am Harshil Shah, a Senior Full Stack Engineer with 14 years of experience delivering software across backend platforms, web applications, enterprise systems, and cloud-enabled products. My work sits at the intersection of engineering quality, product delivery, and architecture. I focus on building systems that are reliable in production, clear to maintain, and designed to evolve with changing business needs.

My primary technology stack includes .NET Core, C#, Python, React, Azure, and AWS. Over the course of my career, I have worked on solutions that required strong technical foundations, thoughtful design trade-offs, and a practical understanding of how software behaves in real operational environments.

docs.title: Award highlight
docs.text:
Asian Banker Award

One of the standout milestones in my career was contributing to the HRMS platform for Axis Bank, which received the Asian Banker Award for Best Retail Banking Software. It remains a strong example of delivering meaningful business impact through solid engineering execution.

docs.title: Current focus
docs.text:
Today, I am most interested in work that sits at the higher end of engineering ownership:

  • Designing maintainable systems with clear boundaries and pragmatic abstractions.
  • Raising engineering standards through reviews, documentation, and technical leadership.
  • Improving delivery confidence with better testing, observability, and release practices.
  • Helping teams make sound architectural decisions without overengineering.

I am particularly aligned with Senior Engineer, Lead Engineer, and Staff Engineer opportunities in the following markets:

  • United Kingdom
  • Netherlands
  • Germany
  • Ireland

These roles are most compelling when they combine hands-on technical work with architecture, mentoring, and engineering culture.

docs.title: Working style
docs.text: I value clarity over noise, simplicity over accidental complexity, and steady delivery over performative activity. The playbook in this repository reflects those values and acts as a practical extension of how I think about software engineering as a craft and as a profession.

docs.title: Architecture Decision Records
docs.text: ADRs help teams preserve the reasoning behind meaningful technical decisions.

docs.title: Suggested template
docs.text:
# ADR: Decision title

## Status
Accepted

## Context
What problem are we solving?

## Decision
What did we choose?

## Consequences
What improves, what becomes harder, and what follow-up work exists?

docs.title: When to create an ADR
docs.text:
• Choosing between competing architectural options
  • Introducing a new platform capability
  • Setting a pattern other teams may follow
  • Making a decision that is hard to reverse later

docs.title: Code Review Guidelines
docs.text: Code review is a quality practice and a collaboration practice.

docs.title: Review for
docs.text:
• Correctness
  • Maintainability
  • Security and privacy concerns
  • Performance impact
  • Test coverage and release safety
  • Naming, clarity, and documentation

docs.title: Reviewer mindset
docs.text: Aim to improve the code and help the author. Be direct, respectful, and specific about risks or trade-offs.

docs.title: Author checklist
docs.text:
• Keep pull requests focused.
  • Explain context and impact.
  • Add tests or justify why they are not needed.
  • Highlight risky areas and rollback considerations.

docs.title: Engineering Principles
docs.text: These are the principles I return to when making technical and delivery decisions.

docs.title: Principles I optimise for
docs.text:
1. Clarity first — systems should be understandable.
  2. Reliability by design — production behaviour matters more than local success.
  3. Simple over clever — complexity must justify itself.
  4. Ownership end to end — build, release, observe, and improve.
  5. Document key decisions — future teams deserve context.
  6. Continuous improvement — every release should teach something.

docs.title: In practice
docs.text: These principles influence architecture, code review, delivery planning, and team collaboration.

docs.title: Python Best Practices
docs.text: Python is most effective when code stays explicit, testable, and easy to reason about.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
• Prefer readability over cleverness.
  • Keep modules focused and functions small.
  • Use type hints for clarity and tooling support.
  • Isolate side effects at the edges.
  • Write tests for behaviour, not implementation details.

docs.title: Production checklist
docs.text:
• Format and lint consistently.
  • Use virtual environments and pinned dependencies when appropriate.
  • Structure packages so imports remain predictable.
  • Log meaningful operational context.
  • Treat scripts as software when they become business critical.

docs.title: SOLID Principles
docs.text: A concise reference for applying SOLID principles in production systems.

docs.title: Single Responsibility Principle
docs.text: Each module or class should have one clear reason to change. Separate domain logic, infrastructure concerns, and presentation behaviour.

docs.title: Open/Closed Principle
docs.text: Prefer extension through composition, interfaces, and configuration instead of modifying stable code paths for every variation.

docs.title: Liskov Substitution Principle
docs.text: Subtypes should honour the contract of their base type. If a replacement changes expected behaviour, the abstraction is wrong.

docs.title: Interface Segregation Principle
docs.text: Design focused interfaces that fit specific consumers. Small interfaces reduce accidental coupling and make testing easier.

docs.title: Dependency Inversion Principle
docs.text: Depend on abstractions that represent behaviour, not concrete implementation details. This improves testability and adaptability.

docs.title: Practical checklist
docs.text:
• Keep classes small and cohesive.
  • Inject dependencies at boundaries.
  • Favour composition over inheritance.
  • Validate abstractions with real use cases.
  • Refactor when behaviour starts spreading across layers.

docs.title: System Design Patterns
docs.text: This section captures practical patterns I rely on when designing systems that need to scale, remain observable, and evolve safely.

docs.title: Core areas
docs.text:
• Define clear service boundaries.
  • Design for failure and recovery.
  • Use asynchronous communication where it reduces coupling.
  • Build observability into the first version.
  • Optimise for maintainability before premature scale.

docs.title: Patterns to revisit
docs.text: Pattern When to use it API Gateway Centralise cross-cutting concerns for external access Event-driven workflows Decouple services and improve resilience Outbox pattern Preserve reliability when publishing integration events Caching Reduce latency and protect downstream dependencies Circuit breaker Prevent cascading failures during partial outages

docs.title: Decision lens
docs.text: Evaluate trade-offs in terms of complexity, reliability, operability, and team cognitive load.
